title: Chess: Attack and Checkmate
description: Build deep intuition for checkmating — from board vision to forcing moves, piece coordination, and mating nets.
subject: chess

chapters.title: The Fundamentals
chapters.description: What is the goal of chess, and how do you recognize when a king is in danger?

lessons.title: What Is the Goal of Chess?
lessons.description: Checkmate — and how to recognize it.

steps.title: The only objective: checkmate
steps.body:
Chess has one objective: **checkmate** the enemy king.

Checkmate means three things are true at the same time:
1. The king is in **check** — it is under attack by an enemy piece
2. The king **cannot move** to any safe square
3. The check **cannot be blocked** or the attacking piece **cannot be captured**

When all three hold, the game ends immediately. Let's look at the three most fundamental checkmates so you can see exactly what this feels like.

steps.title: A king with nowhere to run

stages.body: The black king on a8 is in **check** from the white bishop on c6 (along the diagonal c6–b7–a8).
The king would like to escape. What about **b8**?
The white **knight** on a6 controls b8 — that square is **not available**.
What about **a7**? The king would try to step there…
The white king on b6 controls **a7** — blocked. What about **b7**?

**b7 is blocked too** — the bishop on c6 and the king on b6 both cover it.

Every escape square is watched. The king has no legal move. That is **checkmate** — the game ends here.

steps.question: White to move. The black king is trapped behind its own pawns. Find checkmate in 1.

0.Re7: Re7 does not give check — the king can still move. The rook needs to reach the 8th rank to attack the king directly.
0.Re6: Re6 moves toward the center, but the king can still escape. Find the square that delivers check along rank 8.
0.Re2: A quiet move — no check, no checkmate. Look for a move that attacks the king immediately.

steps.explanation: Re8# — the rook swings to the 8th rank and delivers check to the king on g8. The king cannot escape: f8 and h8 are covered by the rook along rank 8, and f7, g7, h7 are all blocked by Black's own pawns. This is called the **back rank mate** — the king's pawn shelter becomes its prison.
steps.successMessage: The back rank mate — the king's own pawns become its prison!

steps.title: The smothered mate: trapped by your own pieces
steps.body:
A **smothered mate** is delivered by a **knight** to a king that is completely surrounded by its own pieces.

Knights are unique — they jump over other pieces and deliver checks that **cannot be blocked**. If the king has no escape squares because its own army fills them, it is hopelessly trapped.

Look at the board. The black king on h8 is hemmed in on all sides:
- **g8** — blocked by the black rook
- **g7** — blocked by a black pawn
- **h7** — blocked by a black pawn

The white knight on g5 is one move from delivering an unstoppable check. Can you see where it should go?

steps.question: White to move. The black king is smothered by its own pieces. Find the knight checkmate in 1.

0.Nh7: Nh7 gives check, but the king can move to g8 — the rook doesn't block it in this line. Find the square where the knight covers h8 AND blocks all other exits.
0.Ne6: Ne6 doesn't give check at all. The knight needs to jump to a square that attacks h8 directly.
0.Nh3: Nh3 retreats — no check, no progress. Look for the attacking move.

steps.explanation: Nf7# — the knight jumps to f7, attacking the king on h8. Knight checks can never be blocked, only the king can respond. But every escape square is filled by Black's own pieces: g8 has the rook, g7 and h7 have pawns. The king is smothered. Checkmate!
steps.successMessage: The smothered mate — the knight seals the king in its own fortress!

options.text: The king is in check and cannot escape
options.explanation: Exactly right. Check + no legal escape = checkmate. The game ends immediately.

options.text: The king is captured
options.explanation: The king is never actually captured in chess. The game ends the moment the king cannot escape check — before capture.

options.text: A player has no pieces left
options.explanation: You can have many pieces and still be checkmated. It's purely about the king's position.

options.text: A player runs out of moves
options.explanation: Having no moves is only checkmate if you're also in check. If you have no moves but aren't in check, that's stalemate — a draw.

chapters.title: Checkmating Patterns
chapters.description: The essential mating patterns every chess player must know.

lessons.title: The Back Rank Mate
lessons.description: One of the most common checkmates — a rook or queen delivers mate on the first or eighth rank.

steps.title: The back rank mate
steps.body:
The **back rank mate** happens when a rook or queen delivers checkmate on the first or eighth rank, and the enemy king is trapped behind its own pawns.

The pawns on g7, h7, and f7 form a **pawn shelter** — normally protective, but here they become a **prison**. The king has nowhere to go.

Look at the position below. White plays Re8#. The black king on g8 is mated: g7, h7, and f7 are all blocked by Black's own pawns. The king cannot move, and cannot capture the rook (it's protected).

steps.title: Watch the back rank mate unfold
steps.description: Step through this sequence to see how a rook invades the back rank and delivers checkmate.

moveAnnotations.0: Qd8+ — White sacrifices the queen! The black king is already trapped — it can't escape to f8 or h8 (both covered by the rook), and f7/g7/h7 are blocked by its own pawns. Black must take.
moveAnnotations.2: Rxd8# — The second rook sweeps up the d-file to d8, capturing the black rook and delivering checkmate. The king still cannot escape — rank 8 is now controlled by this rook, and all 7th rank squares remain blocked.

steps.question: White to move. Deliver checkmate in 1.
steps.explanation: Re8# — the rook lands on e8, giving check. The black king on g8 is trapped. It has a pawn on g7 and the squares on h7 and f7 are blocked. Checkmate.
steps.successMessage: Clean back rank mate! The pawns that shelter the king become its prison.

steps.question: Black to move. Find checkmate in 2.

0.Qh7+: Qh7+ checks the king but there is no continuation that checkmates after

steps.explanation: Qxb1+ sacrifices the rook — the white king cannot escape (g1 is covered by the queen, h2 is covered by the knight, and g2 is covered by white's pawn. The rook must capture back, and white delivers checkmate.
steps.successMessage: The backrank pattern! The king cannot escape, so white is always at the brink of checkmate!

steps.question: Black just captured your queen with Rxb1 — a catastrophic blunder. White to move. Find checkmate in 1.

preMovePosition.annotation: Black captured the queen on b1 — but the queen was bait. Now the d1 rook has a clear path to the back rank, and the white king on g6 covers every escape square on the 7th rank.

0.Rd7: Rd7 doesn't give check — the king on g8 can still move. The rook needs to land on rank 8.
0.Rd2: Rd2 retreats the rook and throws away the immediate checkmate. The back rank is wide open right now!
0.Re1: Re1 captures nothing useful and misses the checkmate. Look for the rook move to the 8th rank.

steps.explanation: Rd8# — the rook swings to the back rank for immediate checkmate. The black king on g8 is trapped: f8 and h8 are covered by the rook along rank 8, g7 is blocked by Black's own pawn, and f7 and h7 are both covered by the white king on g6. Rxb1 was a classic back rank oversight — Black won a queen but walked into a forced mate.
steps.successMessage: Back rank mate! The queen was a decoy — the rook on d1 was the real threat all along.

lessons.title: Common Mating Patterns
lessons.description: Four essential checkmating patterns every chess player must recognise — from the lawnmower to the epaulette.

steps.title: Why study checkmates first?
steps.body:
Here is a counterintuitive truth about chess improvement: **study the ending before the beginning.**

It doesn't matter if you outplay your opponent and win every piece off the board — if you can't deliver checkmate, the game is a draw. Many beginners win material and then flounder when it's time to finish.

The solution: **study chess backwards.** Learn how to beat your opponent in simplified endgame positions first. Once you know how to deliver checkmate with common material combinations, you develop a concrete goal to aim for throughout the whole game.

Then, and only then, move on to the more complex strategies and dynamics that play out in the middlegame and the opening. The path to chess mastery starts at the end — with checkmate.

steps.title: What are mating patterns — and why learn them?
steps.body:
A **mating pattern** is a recurring arrangement of pieces that reliably forces checkmate. Thousands of different games end in a handful of identical-looking configurations.

Learning these patterns gives you three advantages:
1. **Speed** — you spot checkmate opportunities in seconds instead of calculating from scratch.
2. **Confidence** — you know what the winning position looks like, so you steer toward it.
3. **Economy** — you stop wasting moves and finish games efficiently.

In this lesson you will learn four essential patterns:
1. **The Rook Ladder** — two rooks herd a lone king to the edge, rank by rank
2. **The Arabian Mate** — a knight and rook trap a king in the corner
3. **Morphy's Mate** — a bishop and rook combine on the back rank
4. **The Swiss Cheese Mate** — a queen exploits gaps in the pawn structure.

steps.question: Why is studying mating patterns one of the most effective ways to improve at chess?

options.text: They teach you to recognise winning configurations instantly, so you can steer toward them all game
options.explanation: Exactly. Patterns shortcut calculation — once you know what checkmate looks like, you play toward it from the very first move. That's why strong players make mating attacks look inevitable rather than accidental.

options.text: Memorising patterns is the only way to win — all chess games are just pattern recall
options.explanation: Not quite. Patterns are a powerful tool, but chess also requires calculation, positional judgment, and creativity. Patterns give you a template; you still need to read the position.

options.text: They help you avoid draws by stalemate
options.explanation: Stalemate awareness is a separate skill. Mating patterns are about recognising checkmate configurations quickly so you can create and execute them in your own games.

options.text: Opening theory is more important, so patterns are mainly for beginners
options.explanation: Actually the opposite is true. Understanding how to finish a game is foundational — once you know the endgame, your opening and middlegame choices become purposeful. Strong players of all levels keep sharpening their pattern recognition.

steps.title: The Rook Ladder
steps.description: Two rooks cut off rank after rank, driving the lone king toward the edge. Step through each move to see the technique.

moveAnnotations.0: Rh5+ — the first rook cuts off rank 5. The king must retreat upward, but rank 5 is now permanently sealed. The second rook is waiting.
moveAnnotations.1: Kb4 — the king flees down the board. It has no choice: every square on rank 5 is covered by the rook.
moveAnnotations.2: Rg4+ — the second rook cuts off rank 4. The key idea: one rook holds the previous rank while the other advances. The king cannot cross back.
moveAnnotations.3: Kc3 — forced to retreat again. The king loses another rank.
moveAnnotations.4: Rh3+ — the first rook leapfrogs forward, cutting off rank 3. The alternating pattern is called the 'lawnmower' — the rooks mow down rank after rank.
moveAnnotations.5: Kd2 — the king is nearly out of room now. Rank 1 is the last refuge.
moveAnnotations.6: Rg2+ — rank 2 is sealed. One more rank and the king is cornered.
moveAnnotations.7: Ke1 — the king steps onto rank 1 — there is nowhere left to go.
moveAnnotations.8: Rh1# — the second rook delivers the final blow on rank 1. The king is trapped: rank 2 is controlled by the other rook, and rank 1 is now sealed. Checkmate!

steps.question: White to move. The black king is in the corner. Use the knight and rook to deliver the Arabian Mate in 1.

0.Ra8: Ra8 gives check along rank 8, but the king can escape to g7. You need the rook on a square that also seals rank 7.
0.Rg7: Rg7 controls rank 7 but doesn't give check — the king isn't attacked. The rook needs to be on the h-file to check along it.
0.Nh7: Nh7 attacks the king on h8, but after Kxh7 the king escapes. The knight alone cannot deliver checkmate here.

steps.explanation: Rh7# — the rook slides to h7, checking the king along the h-file. The king has no escape: g8 is covered by the knight on f6, g7 is covered by the rook along rank 7, and capturing the rook is impossible because the knight on f6 protects h7. This is the Arabian Mate — a knight and rook trap the king in the corner.
steps.successMessage: The Arabian Mate — knight guards the escape, rook delivers the final check!

steps.question: White to move. The black king is sheltered by its own pawns — but those pawns are now a prison. Find Morphy's Mate in 1.

0.Rd7: Rd7 doesn't give check — the king can still move. The rook needs to land on rank 8 to attack the king directly.
0.Bf8: Bf8 attacks nothing critical and doesn't give check. The bishop is already perfectly placed on h6 covering g7.
0.Rd6: Rd6 is a quiet move with no immediate threat. Look for the move that delivers check along the back rank.

steps.explanation: Rd8# — the rook lands on the 8th rank and checks the king on g8. Every escape is sealed: f8 and h8 are covered by the rook along rank 8, the pawn on f7 blocks f7, the pawn on h7 blocks h7, and — crucially — g7 is covered by the bishop on h6. Without the bishop, the king could flee to g7. This is Morphy's Mate: rook on the back rank, bishop guarding the key escape diagonal.
steps.successMessage: Morphy's Mate — the bishop seals the diagonal, the rook finishes the job!

steps.question: White to move. Black's pawn structure has holes like Swiss cheese — dark squares g6, g7, and h8 are all weak. Find the queen checkmate in 1.

0.Qh8: Qh8 gives check, but the king can move to f7 or h7 — not checkmate.
0.Qf8: Qf8 gives check, but the king escapes to h7. The queen needs to cover both g8 and the king's escape squares simultaneously.
0.Qg6: Qg6 attacks the pawn but gives no check. Look for the square where the queen delivers check AND seals every escape.

steps.explanation: Qg7# — the queen steps onto the weak g7 square and delivers checkmate. The black king on g8 has nowhere to go: f8 and h8 are covered by the queen along rank 8 and diagonals, f7 and h7 are blocked by Black's own pawns, and g7 is occupied by the queen itself. The black pawn structure on g6, g7, h7 looked like shelter — but it became a prison. The 'Swiss cheese' name comes from the holes punched through Black's dark squares.
steps.successMessage: The Swiss Cheese Mate — Black's own pawns sealed the escape!

chapters.title: Checkmating Drills
chapters.description: Practice delivering checkmate from random positions — track your move count and beat your record.

lessons.title: Checkmate with Queen and King
lessons.description: Herd the lone king to the edge and deliver checkmate. How few moves can you do it in?

steps.title: Box the king in
steps.body:
When you have a queen and king vs a lone king, checkmate is always possible — but you must be systematic.

The strategy has two parts:
1. **Box the king in** — use the queen to shrink the enemy king's available squares, pushing it toward a corner or edge.
2. **Bring your king** — the queen alone cannot force checkmate. Your king must approach to deliver the final blow.

The key insight: **the king must be on the edge or in a corner to be mated.** You cannot checkmate a king in the center of the board.
steps.analogy: Think of the queen as a sheepdog and the enemy king as a sheep. You're herding it to the fence (the edge of the board) before the final roundup.

steps.question: Queen and king vs lone king — which is true about delivering checkmate?

options.text: The enemy king must be on the edge or in a corner
options.explanation: Correct. A king in the center has too many escape squares. Push it to the edge, then the corner, then mate.

options.text: The queen can force checkmate without the king's help
options.explanation: The queen alone cannot deliver checkmate — it needs the king to cut off escape squares near the final position.

options.text: Checkmate can happen anywhere on the board
options.explanation: Not quite. The enemy king must be restricted to the edge or corner. A king in the center has too many flight squares.

options.text: You only need 5 moves maximum
options.explanation: The queen + king vs lone king can take up to 10 moves. The key is being systematic, not fast.

steps.question: Now put it into practice. Deliver checkmate against the bot — it plays random legal moves. How few moves can you do it in?
steps.objective: Checkmate the bot — your move count is shown when you win
steps.explanation: Box the king toward a corner: (1) use the queen to shrink the king's space rank by rank, (2) bring your king in close, (3) force the king to the edge or corner, (4) deliver checkmate — carefully avoiding stalemate. A clean result is under 15 moves.

lessons.title: Checkmate with Rook and King
lessons.description: Harder than the queen — the rook alone cannot force mate. You need precise king coordination.

steps.title: The rook needs its king
steps.body:
Checkmating with a rook and king is one of the most important endgame techniques in chess. Unlike the queen, the rook alone **cannot force the enemy king to the edge** — your king must do the confining work.

The two-step process:
1. **Cut off the king** — use the rook to restrict the enemy king to a shrinking box of ranks or files.
2. **Drive it to the edge** — bring your king in to tighten the box, then deliver the final check on the back rank.

Key rule: **avoid stalemate**. A lone king has very few moves near the edge, so always check you're not accidentally leaving it with no legal move before you deliver check.
steps.analogy: The rook is the fence, your king is the sheepdog. The fence cuts off files and ranks; the dog chases the sheep into the corner.

steps.question: Why is checkmate with a rook harder than with a queen?

options.text: The rook cannot confine the king alone — you need your king to help tighten the box
options.explanation: Exactly. The queen can cut off both ranks and files simultaneously; the rook can only cut off one at a time, so your king must close off the other dimension.

options.text: The rook is weaker than the queen, so it can't give check
options.explanation: Rooks give check just fine. The difficulty is that one rook can't confine the king as efficiently as a queen — your king's help is essential.

options.text: You need two rooks to checkmate a lone king
options.explanation: One rook and a king is enough — but it takes more moves and technique than a queen. Two rooks is even easier (the lawnmower method).

options.text: Stalemate is impossible with a rook, so it's actually easier
options.explanation: Stalemate is very much possible with a rook — more so than with a queen, in fact, because the king near the edge has very few moves. Always check before giving check.

steps.question: Checkmate the bot using your rook and king. The bot plays random legal moves. How few moves can you do it in?
steps.objective: Checkmate the bot — your move count is shown when you win
steps.explanation: The method: (1) use the rook to cut off ranks, shrinking the black king's space, (2) bring your king toward the black king to restrict its movement further, (3) once the king is on the edge, manoeuvre for the final check — watching carefully for stalemate. A strong result is under 20 moves.

chapters.title: Board Vision
chapters.description: Train your eye to see every threat — find all available checkmates.

lessons.title: Find All the Checkmates
lessons.description: Train your board vision: find every square where the queen can deliver checkmate.

steps.title: Board vision: how many checkmates can you see?
steps.body:
Strong players don't just find *a* winning move — they see *all* of them.

This is called **board vision**: the ability to scan the entire board and spot every tactical opportunity at once.

In the position below, the white queen can deliver checkmate in **six different ways** — all in one move. The knight and king hold every escape square for the black king while the queen can strike from multiple directions.

Your task: find all six. Each time you find one, the board resets to the starting position so you can find the next. See how many you spot before needing to guess!

steps.question: White to move. Find ALL 6 checkmates in this position — each one is a different queen move. The board resets after each find.
steps.explanation: All 6 queen moves deliver checkmate because the knight on c6 permanently controls b8, and the king on b6 permanently controls a7 and b7. That traps the black king in the corner regardless of where the queen goes. The queen can strike along rank 8 (Qf8, Qg8, Qh8), the a-file (Qa1), or rank 7 (Qb7, Qa7) — all 6 are forced checkmates!
steps.successMessage: Excellent board vision! You found all 6 checkmates.

chapters.title: Attacking
chapters.description: Learn how checkmates appear in real games — from opening traps to devastating attacks.

lessons.title: The Scholar's Mate
lessons.description: One of the fastest checkmates in chess — delivered in just 4 moves.

steps.title: Checkmates can happen fast
steps.body:
In a real game, checkmate can arrive in just a handful of moves — if your opponent isn't paying attention.

The **Scholar's Mate** (sometimes called the Fool's Mate) is one of the most well-known quick checkmates. White uses the queen and bishop together to attack f7, the weakest square in Black's starting position — defended only by the king.

Step through the game below to see the trap close. Pay attention to how the queen and bishop coordinate: neither piece alone could deliver mate, but together they make it inevitable.

steps.title: The Scholar's Mate
steps.description: White delivers checkmate in 4 moves. Step through each move to see the trap — and the critical mistake.

moveAnnotations.0: 1. e4 — White opens with the King's Pawn, claiming the center and clearing paths for the queen and bishop.
moveAnnotations.1: 1...e5 — Black mirrors, also staking a claim in the center. The position looks perfectly equal.
moveAnnotations.2: 2. Qh5 — White brings the queen out early to h5. Unusual — but it immediately eyes f7, defended only by Black's king. A threat is brewing.
moveAnnotations.3: 2...Nc6 — Black develops the knight, defending e5. Sensible, but the threat on f7 is still unaddressed. 2...g6 (pushing the queen back) was the correct response.
moveAnnotations.4: 3. Bc4 — The bishop aims at f7. Now the queen on h5 and bishop on c4 both target f7. The trap is fully set.
moveAnnotations.5: 3...Nf6?? — A natural-looking developing move, but a fatal blunder. Black tries to attack the queen without realising White has checkmate in one. The only defence was 3...g6.
moveAnnotations.6: 4. Qxf7# — Checkmate! The queen lands on f7, protected by the bishop on c4. The king cannot escape: d7 and e7 are controlled by the queen along rank 7, f8 is on the f-file, and d8 is blocked by Black's own queen. The bishop prevents capturing the queen.

2.label: Qh5 — eyes f7
2.detail: The queen on h5 immediately eyes **f7**, defended only by Black's king.

4.label: Bc4 — aims at f7
4.detail: The bishop on c4 and queen on h5 both target **f7**.

steps.question: Why is 3...Nf6 a blunder in the Scholar's Mate?

options.text: It allows Qxf7# — checkmate that cannot be stopped
options.explanation: Exactly. After Nf6, White has an immediate forced checkmate with Qxf7#. The queen is protected by the bishop, and the king has no escape. Black should have played 3...g6 to push the queen back.

options.text: It loses the knight to a bishop fork
options.explanation: No fork is involved here. The issue is that Nf6 ignores the checkmate threat on f7. The queen and bishop already had checkmate set up.

options.text: It weakens the pawn structure
options.explanation: Pawn structure is irrelevant here — the game ends on the very next move with Qxf7#. The problem is the immediate checkmate threat that Nf6 fails to address.

options.text: It blocks the bishop on f8
options.explanation: While Nf6 does block the f8 bishop, that's not the critical problem. The real issue is that it does nothing to stop Qxf7#, which is checkmate.

chapters.title: Advanced
chapters.description: Complex positions requiring deep calculation and positional understanding — for developing players.

lessons.title: Choosing the Right Move
lessons.description: Four candidate moves, one winner — understand the difference between good and great.

steps.title: Why candidate moves matter
steps.body:
At the advanced level, it's rarely the case that only one move exists. Often there are **four or five plausible moves**, all of which seem reasonable at first glance.

This is where chess gets interesting. An engine can tell you the best move — but it cannot explain *why* that move is better than the others. That understanding comes from experience with patterns, piece coordination, and a feel for what the position demands.

In the next puzzle, you'll be given four candidate moves. Try each one on the board, think about why it might or might not work, and find the move that gives White the most decisive advantage.

steps.question: White to move. Which is the best among these four candidate moves? Click each one to see it played on the board, then select the strongest.

candidates.explanation: Rf8 looks active, but it gives Black time to seize the initiative. Black can respond with Qd1+, then follow up with Qh5+, giving a perpetual check — at minimum a draw. Alternatively, Black simply plays Rxf8 and White has no good continuation. A decisive attacking idea must be more forcing.

Well spotted that a check is the most forcing type of move! However, Qe7+ doesn't lead anywhere decisive. After Kxe7, White's queen is exchanged and the position simplifies in Black's favour. After Rxe7, White again has no follow-up — the rooks and queen are not coordinated for a mating attack. Forcing moves must force *something concrete*.

Qxf7+ is a natural try: it's a queen check and captures material. However, after Black recaptures (Rxf7 or Kxf7), the queens come off and White doesn't have enough pieces left to mount a decisive attack on the Black king. Tactics need to lead to a clearly won endgame or mate — winning material isn't enough if the opponent can defend.

steps.successMessage: Rxg6+! The rook removes the key defensive pawn on g6 with check, displacing the Black king and opening lines for White's queen and rook to co-ordinate for a decisive attack.
